---
title: "Exploring Risk Gradients"
author: "Andy"
date: "2025-06-30"
output: pdf_document
---

This document contains an attempt to explore how the relative contribution of NCEs to the total predator effect varies across a risk gradient. This is similar to Fig. 4 in the grant proposal, which uses predator density as a proxy for risk.

![Fig. 4 from Grant Proposal](grantfig4.png)

Notably, the curves generally reach a saturation point, where the contribution of NCEs to the total predator effect levels off with increasing risk. One goal of this document is determining why the percentage of the total predator effect composed of the NCE generally levels off given increasing risk.

\pagebreak

# Exploring the Mechanisms

We will first recreate curve A in the above figure, but on a finer scale. Specifically, we will look at the underlying IREF curves and their shapes to determine why this relationship is saturating.

```{r, warning = FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Setting the range of prey displacement rate
trait.vec <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
speed.vec <- 1 - trait.vec

# These formulas are simplified by setting some of the values as constants
# r is always assumed to be 1, while k, s, and v are manipulated values
pred.curve <- function(k, v, s){
  k*sqrt(v^2 + s^2)
}

# R is always assumed to be 1, while s and h are manipulated values
growth.curve <- function(s, R, h){
  (R * s)/(1 + s*R*h)
}

k_vec <- c(0, 0.1, 0.13, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 1) # Range of values used by Scott for Fig. 4 in the grant proposal
h_val <- 8
R_val <- 1
cost_constant_val <- 9
v_val <- 0.2
pred_constant_val <- 0.98058

# Generate growth and mortality curves for each value of predator density (k_vec)
fig4.df <- as.data.frame( mat.or.vec( length( trait.vec ) * length(k_vec), 7) )
colnames(fig4.df) <- c("trait", "speed", "growth_curve", "mort_curve", "fitness", "pct_NCE", "pred_dens")

row <- 0

for (i in 1:length(k_vec)){
  for (j in 1:length(trait.vec)){
    row <- row + 1
    fig4.df[row, 1] <- trait.vec[j]
    fig4.df[row, 2] <- speed.vec[j]
    fig4.df[row, 3] <- cost_constant_val * growth.curve( s = fig4.df[row, 2], R = R_val, h = h_val )
    fig4.df[row, 4] <- pred_constant_val * pred.curve( k = k_vec[i], v = v_val, s = fig4.df[row, 2] )
    fig4.df[row, 5] <- fig4.df[row, 3] - fig4.df[row, 4]
    fig4.df[row, 6] <- (1 - fig4.df[row, 3])/((1 - fig4.df[row, 3]) + fig4.df[row, 4])
    fig4.df[row, 7] <- k_vec[i]
  }
}
```

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Summarize data by finding optimum fitness for each value of predator density
optim.df <- fig4.df %>%
  group_by( pred_dens ) %>%
  slice( which.max(fitness) )

fig4.df$pred_dens <- as.factor(fig4.df$pred_dens)
optim.df[1, 6] <- 0 # Removing an NA created by dividing by zero where pred_dens = 0


# Plot % contribution of NCE to total predator effect by predator density
ggplot( data = optim.df, aes( x = pred_dens ) ) +
  geom_point( aes( y = pct_NCE ) ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 10, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("% NCE contribution to") +
  xlab("Predator Density") +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1)
```

First, NCE contributions to the total predator effect, to ensure that this was done similarly to Fig. 4 in the grant proposal above. This shows the same saturating relationship, which is good news. Next, we'll look under the hood at the underlying IREF curves.

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Plot IREF curves by predator density
ggplot( data = fig4.df, aes( x = trait ) ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = growth_curve ), color = "blue" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = mort_curve ), color = "red" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = fitness ), color = "green" ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 7, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("Growth and Mortality") +
  xlab("Displacement Rate") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1), labels = c(1000, 500, 1) ) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1) ) +
  facet_wrap(~pred_dens) +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1 )
```

The above figure shows the growth (in blue), mortality (in red), and fitness (in green) curves, as a function of prey displacement rate. Each plot represents a different predator density, noted in the header above the plot.

The reason that the NCE's contribution to the total predator effect tends to level off with increasing risk seems to occur for two main reasons. 

The optimal RITR seems to occur roughly around the inflection point of the growth curve, except when predator density (and therefore risk) is low (k > 0.2) and a weak response is expected by the prey.

Put another way, as risk increases, there is eventually a point at which the benefit of the RITR (in terms of reduction of mortality due to predation) becomes too costly. Quantitatively, this is probably where the slope of the growth curve exceeds greatly exceeds the slope of the mortality curve.

This finding is probably general in cases where the growth curve is concave-down, while predation risk declines in a linear or saturating way.

\pagebreak

Here is a table showing the optimal trait and speed values, and the percentage of the total predator effect consisting of NCEs, as a function of predator density.

```{r, warning=FALSE}
optim.df[ -c(3:5) ]
```

Incidentally, the inflection point of the growth curve is at around a displacement rate of 375 (calculated below using the R package 'inflection').

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
library(inflection)

x <- speed.vec
y <- cost_constant_val * growth.curve( s = speed.vec, R = R_val, h = h_val )
cc=check_curve(x,y);cc
ede(x, y, cc$index)
```

The inflection point lies at the value labeled "chi". Note that this is the trait value, so to get the displacement rate you must subtract it from 1, then multiply by 1000.

\pagebreak

# Other Fitness Optimization Functions

Out of curiosity, what if we had chosen to optimize fitness in the above example using $\mu/g$? Do we see the same general behavior of the optimum, and the proportion of the total predator effect that comes from NCEs?

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Calculate mu/g from the above data frame
fig4.df$mu_g <- fig4.df$mort_curve/fig4.df$growth_curve

# Plot mu/g by predator density
ggplot( data = fig4.df, aes( x = trait ) ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = mu_g ), color = "green" ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 7, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("mu/g") +
  xlab("Displacement Rate") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1), labels = c(1000, 500, 1) ) + 
  facet_wrap(~pred_dens) +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1 )

optim2.df <- fig4.df %>%
  group_by( pred_dens ) %>%
  slice( which.max(fitness) )

optim2.df[ -c(3:6) ]
```

The optimal displacement rate (2nd column) is exactly the same as before.

\pagebreak

# What if the Predator has a Type II FR?

Next, I want to explore whether the above finding would change if the predator had a Type II functional response, as is typical for most predators. This may help to establish whether this finding is "general" to the majority of predator-prey systems. We'll start with an (arbitrary) predator handling time of 5.

```{r, warning=FALSE}

# First, write a new Type II mortality curve
# This function will take the above encounter rate formula and apply handling time to it
typeII.curve <- function( a_pred, h_pred ){
  a_pred / ( 1 + a_pred * h_pred )
}

k_vec <- c(0, 0.1, 0.13, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 1) # Range of values used by Scott for Fig. 4 in the grant proposal
h_val <- 8
h_pred_val <- 5 # Start at a predator handling time value of 5
R_val <- 1
cost_constant_val <- 9
v_val <- 0.2
pred_constant_val <- 5

# Generate growth and mortality curves for each value of predator density (k_vec)
typeII.df <- as.data.frame( mat.or.vec( length( trait.vec ) * length(k_vec), 7) )
colnames(typeII.df) <- c("trait", "speed", "growth_curve", "mort_curve", "fitness", "pct_NCE", "pred_dens")

row <- 0

for (i in 1:length(k_vec)){
  for (j in 1:length(trait.vec)){
    row <- row + 1
    typeII.df[row, 1] <- trait.vec[j]
    typeII.df[row, 2] <- speed.vec[j]
    typeII.df[row, 3] <- cost_constant_val * growth.curve( s = typeII.df[row, 2], R = R_val, h = h_val )
    typeII.df[row, 4] <- pred_constant_val * typeII.curve( a_pred = pred.curve( k = k_vec[i], v = v_val, s = typeII.df[row, 2]), h_pred = h_pred_val )
    typeII.df[row, 5] <- typeII.df[row, 3] - typeII.df[row, 4]
    typeII.df[row, 6] <- (1 - typeII.df[row, 3])/((1 - typeII.df[row, 3]) + typeII.df[row, 4])
    typeII.df[row, 7] <- k_vec[i]
  }
}
```

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Summarize data by finding optimum fitness for each value of predator density
optim3.df <- typeII.df %>%
  group_by( pred_dens ) %>%
  slice( which.max(fitness) )

typeII.df$pred_dens <- as.factor(typeII.df$pred_dens)
optim3.df[1, 6] <- 0 # Removing an NA created by dividing by zero where pred_dens = 0

# Plot IREF curves by predator density
ggplot( data = typeII.df, aes( x = trait ) ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = growth_curve ), color = "blue" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = mort_curve ), color = "red" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = fitness ), color = "green" ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 7, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("Growth and Mortality") +
  xlab("Displacement Rate") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1), labels = c(1000, 500, 1) ) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1) ) +
  facet_wrap(~pred_dens) +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1 )
```

The behavior of the fitness curves is similar looking...

```{r, warning=FALSE,echo=FALSE}
optim3.df[ -c(3:5) ]
```

And the optimums are for the most part in a similar place (except at very high predator densities!), but...

\pagebreak

```{r, warning=FALSE,echo=FALSE}
# Plot % contribution of NCE to total predator effect by predator density
ggplot( data = optim3.df, aes( x = pred_dens ) ) +
  geom_point( aes( y = pct_NCE ) ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 10, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("% NCE contribution to") +
  xlab("Predator Density") +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1)
```

The relative contribution of the NCE to the total predator effect has a very different behavior. It peaks when predator density is low, and then declines with increasing predator density. I think this is quantitatively because the position of the mortality curve as a whole -- but especially at the far right end of the plots (i.e., where displacement rate is very low) -- shifts upwards as predator density increases, which will always increase the impact of CEs.

Biologically, I think this is because at very low prey displacement rates, predation rates start to increase again because of the effect of handling time. This acts to push the optimal displacement rate back to the left, because if encounters with predators are infrequent enough, those predators will always be hungry.

Last, we'll revisit the above finding from the Type I FRs. To summarize, increasing predation risk drives the optimum to the right, but this rightward shift is limited by the costs to growth becoming too steep. That seems to be the case here too, but with an added wrinkle: if risk is sufficiently high and encounters with the predator become too infrequent, that can shift the optimal displacement rate back to the left.

\pagebreak

# Predator Foraging Mode

Another important component of our manuscript is the impact of predator foraging mode on the optimal RITR for prey. Let's quickly explore how the effects of the above risk gradient differ for an active predator.

```{r, warning=FALSE}
k_vec <- c(0, 0.1, 0.13, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 1) # Range of values used by Scott for Fig. 4 in the grant proposal
h_val <- 8
R_val <- 1
cost_constant_val <- 9
v_val <- 1
pred_constant_val <- 0.7071068 * 0.8999994

# Generate growth and mortality curves for each value of predator density (k_vec)
predmode.df <- as.data.frame( mat.or.vec( length( trait.vec ) * length(k_vec), 7) )
colnames(predmode.df) <- c("trait", "speed", "growth_curve", "mort_curve", "fitness", "pct_NCE", "pred_dens")

row <- 0

for (i in 1:length(k_vec)){
  for (j in 1:length(trait.vec)){
    row <- row + 1
    predmode.df[row, 1] <- trait.vec[j]
    predmode.df[row, 2] <- speed.vec[j]
    predmode.df[row, 3] <- cost_constant_val * growth.curve( s = predmode.df[row, 2], R = R_val, h = h_val )
    predmode.df[row, 4] <- pred_constant_val * pred.curve( k = k_vec[i], v = v_val, s = predmode.df[row, 2] )
    predmode.df[row, 5] <- predmode.df[row, 3] - predmode.df[row, 4]
    predmode.df[row, 6] <- (1 - predmode.df[row, 3])/((1 - predmode.df[row, 3]) + predmode.df[row, 4])
    predmode.df[row, 7] <- k_vec[i]
  }
}
```

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Summarize data by finding optimum fitness for each value of predator density
optim4.df <- predmode.df %>%
  group_by( pred_dens ) %>%
  slice( which.max(fitness) )

predmode.df$pred_dens <- as.factor(predmode.df$pred_dens)
optim4.df[1, 6] <- 0 # Removing an NA created by dividing by zero where pred_dens = 0


# Plot % contribution of NCE to total predator effect by predator density
ggplot( data = optim4.df, aes( x = pred_dens ) ) +
  geom_point( aes( y = pct_NCE ) ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 10, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("% NCE contribution to") +
  xlab("Predator Density") +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1)
```

```{r, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Plot IREF curves by predator density
ggplot( data = predmode.df, aes( x = trait ) ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = growth_curve ), color = "blue" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = mort_curve ), color = "red" ) +
  geom_line( aes( y = fitness ), color = "green" ) +
  theme_classic( base_size = 7, base_line_size = 1 ) + 
  ylab("Growth and Mortality") +
  xlab("Displacement Rate") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1), labels = c(1000, 500, 1) ) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1) ) +
  facet_wrap(~pred_dens) +
  theme( panel.border = element_rect( color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.75 ), aspect.ratio = 1 )

optim4.df[ -c(3:5) ]
```

Similar general finding, except that the contribution of the NCE to the total predator effect is pretty low throughout. This is probably because when predators are more active, prey foraging rate matters less.

General finding seems to be, the more risk the further to the right things will go (at least for a Type I FR), whereas, things that contribute to the cost of responding push it back to the left.
